symbols: flames surrounding the figure — purifying fire that cleanses the soul of residual sin and attachment
upward gaze — the soul's yearning for God and the beatific vision, hope even amid torment
bare chest and exposed flesh — vulnerability and the stripping away of all worldly pretense before divine judgment
dark background — spiritual isolation and the darkness of separation from the full presence of God
open mouth — cry of anguish and supplication, the soul calling out for mercy and release
description: A close-up bust portrait of a figure with curly dark hair, bare shoulders and chest, gazing upward with an expression of intense anguish. The mouth is slightly open as if crying out. Flames or fiery wisps of orange and red curl around the figure's head and shoulders, emerging from a deep, dark background. The palette is restricted to warm browns, golds, and orange-red tones against near-black. A small number '35' is inscribed in the lower left corner.
theological_description: The title 'El Alma en Pena' (The Soul in Torment) places this image squarely within Catholic eschatological theology, specifically the doctrine of Purgatory — the state of purification after death for souls destined for heaven but not yet fully cleansed. The Council of Trent (1563) affirmed the existence of Purgatory and the efficacy of prayers for the dead. The figure's upward gaze is theologically significant: unlike the damned in hell who are turned permanently away from God, the soul in Purgatory looks upward with hope, knowing that purification will end in the beatific vision. The flames are understood not as punitive but as purgative — the refining fire of Malachi 3:2-3 that purifies 'the sons of Levi' like gold and silver. Paul's teaching in 1 Corinthians 3:13-15 that each person's work will be tested by fire, and though some may suffer loss, they themselves 'will be saved, but only as through fire,' provides the primary New Testament basis for this doctrine. The figure's exposed vulnerability recalls the stripping of all earthly pretense before the judgment seat of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:10). The intercessory tradition — that the living can aid these souls through prayer, almsgiving, and the sacrifice of the Mass — connects to 2 Maccabees 12:46 and the communion of saints that bridges the Church Militant and the Church Suffering.

scripture_refs.reason: Each one's work will be tested by fire; the one who suffers loss will be saved, but only as through fire — the key New Testament text underlying the doctrine of purgatorial purification.
Jesus says some sins will not be forgiven 'in this age or the age to come,' implying the possibility of post-mortem forgiveness — a text traditionally cited in support of Purgatory.
All must appear before the judgment seat of Christ to receive recompense for deeds done in the body — the vulnerability of the soul before divine scrutiny.
The Lord is like a refiner's fire, purifying the sons of Levi like gold and silver — the Old Testament image of divine purification by fire.
Out of the depths I cry to you, O Lord — the De Profundis, the traditional psalm of the souls in Purgatory, matching the figure's upward cry.
Nothing unclean shall enter the heavenly Jerusalem — the theological rationale for a state of purification before entering God's presence.
